title: Manager resume command fakes a running launch without execution
summary: `/manager resume <worker> run` records `running` launch state and `active` worker state without actually executing and finalizing the worker runtime, leaving durable state inconsistent with reality.
recommendedAction: Make manager resume run the same full launch lifecycle as the tool path or otherwise avoid recording running state until execution truly begins and finishes.
resolutionNotes: `/manager resume ... run` now invokes `runWorkerLaunch()` and `finishLaunchExecution()` so durable state reflects real execution instead of a fictitious running launch.

title: Scheduler excludes legitimately unblocked workers from resume candidates
summary: The scheduler only considers `requested`, `ready`, or `active` workers as resume candidates when unresolved inbox exists, so workers previously blocked/waiting for review stay stranded even after manager messages arrive.
recommendedAction: Broaden resume-candidate logic to include blocked/waiting states that now have actionable inbox backlog and no longer require exclusive manager review.
resolutionNotes: Scheduler resume-candidate logic now includes blocked workers and waiting-for-review workers that are not pending approval, and tests cover the unblocked-worker case.

title: Manager surface cannot resolve manager-owned inbox backlog
summary: The manager control plane exposes overview, supervise, message, approve, and resume, but lacks any action to acknowledge/resolve worker-to-manager escalations or clarifications, leaving `managerInbox` backlog permanently outstanding unless callers drop back to raw worker mutation.
recommendedAction: Add explicit manager-side acknowledge/resolve handling for manager-owned inbox items through the manager surface.
resolutionNotes: Both `/manager` and `manager_write` now expose acknowledge/resolve actions for manager-owned inbox items, and tests prove manager backlog can be durably resolved through the manager surface.

title: lastRuntimeKind is updated at prepare time instead of execution time
summary: Preparing a launch updates `lastRuntimeKind` even when no execution ever occurs, making durable state claim a runtime kind that may never have actually hosted the worker.
recommendedAction: Move `lastRuntimeKind` updates to the point where execution truly starts or finishes rather than descriptor preparation.
resolutionNotes: `lastRuntimeKind` now remains null during prepare-only launches and is set when execution actually starts. Runtime tests cover both SDK execution and prepare-only recovery behavior.
